name: Kismet
short_description: An expressive robotic head built at MIT in the late 1990s to engage people in face-to-face social and emotional exchange.
long_description: Kismet is a disembodied robot head developed by Cynthia Breazeal as her doctoral work under Rodney Brooks at MIT, widely regarded as a founding artifact of social robotics. Using cameras, microphones tuned to infant-directed speech, and a synthetic-nervous-system control architecture, it perceives a human partner and responds with legible affective displays through motorized ears, eyebrows, eyelids, lips, jaw, and neck, plus expressive vocal babble synthesized with DECtalk. Modeled on infant-caregiver interaction, Kismet maintained an internal motivation-and-emotion state that drove turn-taking and proto-conversational engagement rather than completing any external task. It now resides in the MIT Museum.

images.caption: Kismet (1993-2000) on display at the MIT Museum, Cambridge, Massachusetts.
images.alt_text: A robotic head with large round camera eyes, expressive pink rubber lips, mobile eyebrows, and pointed ears mounted on a metal neck, displayed in a museum case.
